coredata.dc:title: Nucleobase chemosensor based on carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Yu
Liu, Xue
Tang, Xiuping
Li, Tianze
Wu, Qiuhua
Jiang, Yuchun
Yi, Jie
Zhang, Guolin
coredata.dc:description: A facile and sensitive fluorescence protocol for nucleobase detection was developed based on carbon nanodot (CD) chemosensors. The novel fluorescent CDs were prepared using four kinds of nucleobases (including adenine, guanine, thymine and cytosine) as separate carbon sources via simple hydrothermal strategy. The quantum yield of adenine CDs (A-CDs), guanine CDs (G-CDs), thymine CDs (T-CDs) and cytosine CDs (C-CDs) was checked as 15.1%, 28.3%, 10.6% and 11.7%, respectively. Four CDs can recognize their complementary nucleobases based on the principle of complementary base pairing. Their fluorescence was linearly quenched with the increase of nucleobase concentrations under optimal conditions. Combining the calibration curve, quantitative assay of nucleobase in solution can be realized. For example, A-CDs could determine thymine in the concentration range of 2–20mM with a detection limit of ca. 0.053mM, and the linear equation is fitting as (I0−I) / I = 0.01961 × CT(mM) + 0.01756 (R2 = 0.994). Thymine can induce the fluorescence lifetime of A-CDs decreasing from 5.58 to 3.34ns, indicating a dynamic quenching mechanism. The novel nucleobase sensors were also evaluated in specific solution environment. A-CDs showed a relatively minor relative standard deviation (< 4.0%) in fetal calf serum solution, indicating a high accuracy and credibility of the sensing system. In view of the excellent sensitivity, preferable biocompatibility as well as simple constructing method, the sensing platform derived from the nucleobase-based CDs present great potential in biological sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Fluorescence
Chemosensor
Nucleobase detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence assay of nucleobases in solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM images of A-CDs. Insets: HRTEM image of A-CDs; b) size distribution of A-CDs; c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of A-CDs; d) Excitation-dependent PL spectra of A-CDs. Inset in (d): normalized PL spectra of the A-CDs.

Fluorescence quenching rate of A-CDs containing different analytes (20mM). (ATP: adenosine triphosphate; ADP: adenosine diphosphate; MELA: melamine; PA: phthalic acid; EDA: ethylenediamine; C: cytosine; G: guanine; U: uracil; T: thymine).
Fluorescence spectra of A-CDs a) with various concentrations of thymine; b) Correlation curve of a) fluorescence quenching rate of A-CDs to thymine concentrations (Inset: the linear region).
Fluorescence decays of the A-CDs with nucleobases.
Schematic illustration of the nucleobase-based CDs.
Thymine assay in PBS solution and FBS solution (10 repeated trials, RSD: relative standard deviation).

head.ce:title: Nucleobase chemosensor based on carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and sensitive fluorescence protocol for nucleobase detection was developed based on carbon nanodot (CD) chemosensors. The novel fluorescent CDs were prepared using four kinds of nucleobases (including adenine, guanine, thymine and cytosine) as separate carbon sources via simple hydrothermal strategy. The quantum yield of adenine CDs (A-CDs), guanine CDs (G-CDs), thymine CDs (T-CDs) and cytosine CDs (C-CDs) was checked as 15.1%, 28.3%, 10.6% and 11.7%, respectively. Four CDs can recognize their complementary nucleobases based on the principle of complementary base pairing. Their fluorescence was linearly quenched with the increase of nucleobase concentrations under optimal conditions. Combining the calibration curve, quantitative assay of nucleobase in solution can be realized. For example, A-CDs could determine thymine in the concentration range of 2–20mM with a detection limit of ca. 0.053mM, and the linear equation is fitting as (I0−I) / I = 0.01961 × CT(mM) + 0.01756 (R2 = 0.994). Thymine can induce the fluorescence lifetime of A-CDs decreasing from 5.58 to 3.34ns, indicating a dynamic quenching mechanism. The novel nucleobase sensors were also evaluated in specific solution environment. A-CDs showed a relatively minor relative standard deviation (< 4.0%) in fetal calf serum solution, indicating a high accuracy and credibility of the sensing system. In view of the excellent sensitivity, preferable biocompatibility as well as simple constructing method, the sensing platform derived from the nucleobase-based CDs present great potential in biological sensing applications.

CDs prepared using nucleobases as carbon sources can quantitively determine their corresponding complementary bases through fluorescence.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Developing sensors for biological analytes has received increasing attention in recent years [1–4]. Rational designing sensors for various bio-analytes can avoid time-consuming synthesis and testing of numerous candidates. However, the complex relations between the function of sensors and their compositions, synthetic method and end-use conditions often make it difficult to apply for real-world applications [5].

Carbon nanodots (CDs), a class of low-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, have aroused a great deal of interest in recent years [6–9]. CDs are of better biocompatibility, as well as convenient and green synthesis, compared with traditional fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots (QDs). Therefore, considerable efforts have been made to use CDs instead of QDs in biological fields [10–14]. CDs can be prepared through chemical reactions using universal carbon sources, from small molecular precursors, carbon materials and natural bioorganic precursors to even food waste [6–8]. Some resultant CDs can inherit crucial functional groups from special carbon sources, which can selectively bind with specific bio-analytes [15,16]. The binding effect can induce energy transfer between the CD sensors and their corresponding analytes, leading to fluorescence signal variations. Quantitative and qualitative analysis of the analytes can be realized according to the fluorescence signal variations of CDs. For example, Wang et al. constructed CDs using phenylboronic acid as single carbon source [15]. The as-prepared CDs carried on boronic acid group from phenylboronic acid. Boronic acid group is one of the most commonly used recognition of functional groups for glucose, so their CDs can be applied in blood glucose sensing. Besides, CDs can also be modified by some functional model recognition units, which endow CDs with corresponding sensing ability [17–19]. Tian et al. conjugated CDs with a specific organic molecule, N-(2-aminoethyl)-N,N,N’tris(pyridin-2-ylmethyl)ethane-1,2-diamine (AE-TPEA), a specific organic molecule sensing for Cu2+ [17]. The as-prepared CDs showed high selectivity to Cu2+ over the other competing metal ions at cellular concentrations. These two sensor constructing methods based on CDs highly simplify the process of sensor design.

Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) is the hereditary material in almost all living organisms and many viruses, which plays a crucial role in the life process. The helical structure of DNA is formed through a well-known example of a structure that results from the molecular recognition properties of the base pairs adenine-thymine (A-T) and cytosine-guanine (C-G) [20]. These nucleobases have long been considered key to the DNA's stability, replication, and capacity for information storage. Nucleobase concentration in physiological environment can effectively reflect the level of oxidative damage to DNA [21,22]. Moreover, in organism, abnormal changes of the nucleobases can also reveal the mutation and deficiency of the immunity system, through which, various diseases have been diagnosed. Therefore, the concentration of nucleobases are considered important for the diagnosis of cancer, AIDS, myocardial cellular energy status, disease progress, and therapy responses [23]. Various analytical methods have been established to determine the nucleobase concentration, including electrochemical methods [24–27], capillary electrophoresis [28,29], and chromatography [30,31]. However, in these methods, complicated and expensive equipment are necessary in chromatography, while in electrochemical methods, exacting and fussy electrode procedures, as well as instable electrodes, usually embarrass the researchers.

Fluorescence methods have advantages in their superior sensitivity and convenient operation, yet few efforts have been devoted to fluorescence method in determining nucleobase concentration [32–35]. Herein, we demonstrated a simple and sensitive fluorescence method for nucleobase detection using CDs as the chemosensor. The functionalized CDs were prepared using one-step synthesis method, employing nucleobases as a single carbon source, without any surface modification. The synthesis procedures and corresponding structure of CDs are presented in Scheme 1. Nucleobases play a dual role in our system: (1) Basically, its molecular constitution serve as carbon source in preparing the CDs. Moreover, their superior N / C ratio can simultaneously provide with rich nitrogen source, which can enhance the quantum yield of the as-prepared CDs through N-doping [36,37]. (2) Crucially, their nucleobase groups can realize one-step “synthesis-modification integration” fabrication, making the as-prepared CDs directly functionalized with nucleobase groups. The added nucleobases can selectively bind with their corresponding complementary nucleobase groups on the surface of CDs through hydrogen bond interaction. The interaction can promote energy transfer between nucleobase-based CDs and their complementary bases, resulting in linear fluorescence quenching effect, according to which, quantitative determination of nucleobases can be realized.

Adenine (98%), guanine (99%), thymine (99%), cytosine (98%) and quinine sulfate dehydrate (98%) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co. LTD. All the other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company Limited (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was prepared with an ionic strength of 50mM and pH = 7.4. Metal ion solutions were prepared from their nitrates with doubly distilled water. Solvents of analytical grade were purchased and used without further purification. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was tested on a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan), equipped with a 200kV accelerating voltage. HRTEM samples were prepared through dropping CD solution onto a 200-mesh copper grid, which was coated with a lacy carbon film. The grain diameter and size distribution of the CDs were gotten using a Malvern Nano ZS instrument. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) tests were performed through a TU-1900 spectrophotometer (Purkinje General Instrument Co. LTD, China). Fluorescence measurements were performed using Cary Eclipse (Varian, America), equipped with a thermostated cell holder to control and regulate temperature. The excitation and emission slit were set as 5nm and 5nm, respectively. The excitation wavelength was set at 320nm unless specially mentioned, and the fluorescence intensity was taken from the maximum emission wavelength. The finally present fluorescence spectra were processed through normalized method, which were obtained by dividing each fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence spectra by the maximum value of their own. The fluorescence lifetime was measured using Fluorolog (Horiba Jobin Yvon) with a TCSPC system.

Adenine CDs (A-CDs) were prepared using hydrothermal method. In a typical process, 0.68g adenine (5mmol) and 10mL NaOH solution (0.5M) were added into a 25mL stainless-steel autoclave equipped with a Teflon liner. The reaction was performed at 180°C for 12h to give out yellow A-CD solution. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 12,000rpm for 20min. The supernatant was taken and dialyzed against ultra-pure water for 48h to remove residual carbon source and NaOH (dialysis membrane molecular weight cutoff = 100 – 500Da, Shanghai Baoman Biological technology Co. LTD). The powder was obtained by vacuum freeze–drying and then redissolved in ultra-pure water. The final concentration of the A-CDs solution (pH = 7) was 3 × 10−5 g / mL. The resultant A-CD solution was maintained at 4°C for further characterization and use.

The preparation of guanine CDs (G-CDs), thymine CDs (T-CDs) and cytosine CDs (C-CDs) were conducted following a similar procedure except for the initial reactants of 0.30g guanine (2mmol), 0.63g thymine (5mmol) and 0.56g cytosine (5mmol), respectively.

The QY of the CDs was monitored using a comparative method [38]. The maximum emission location of CDs is similar to that of quinine sulfate, and therefore we took quinine sulfate as the reference substance to determine the QY of CDs. In literature, the QY of quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 was regarded as 54% [39]. A series of pending test samples were dissolved in ultrapure water to form various concentrations. The absorbance values of these solutions were followed at the maximum emission wavelength (340nm). 10mm fluorescence cuvette was used; thus the absorbance values should never exceed 0.1 to minimize re-absorption effects. Therefore, we prepared a series of pending test samples solutions with the absorbance values from 0 to 0.1, including various CD and quinine sulfate solutions. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission spectra of these solutions were recorded at the maximum emission wavelength (340nm). The fluorescence emission spectra were processed with integration, and the integral area was calculated in the wavelength range from 350 to 600nm. A graph was plotted using the integral area values against the absorbance values, and a trend line was added for each curve with intercept at zero. Absolute values were calculated according to the following equation:
Φ
x
=
Φ
ST
(
Grad
X
Grad
ST
) (
η
X
2
η
ST
2
)
where the subscripts ST and X denote standard and test respectively, Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, Grad is the gradient from the plot of the integral area vs absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of nucleobases in solution
ce:section.ce:para: A series of microliter amounts of nucleobase solutions were mixed with 4mL CD PBS solution to obtain desired nucleobase concentration range (ca. 0–30mM) and mixed even before fluorescence measurements were performed in a 10mm quartz cuvette.

The structure of the nucleobase-based CDs was investigated through transmission electron microscope (TEM), laser particle size analyzer and ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) tests. Adenine-based CDs (A-CDs), thymine-based CDs (T-CDs), guanine-based CDs (G-CDs) and cytosine-based (C-CDs) were the CDs prepared using adenine, thymine, guanine and cytosine as the single carbon source, respectively. Fig. 1a presents typical TEM image of well-dispersed A-CDs. High resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in the inset of Fig. 1a reveals that the lattice space was about 0.23nm, indicating a (1 0 0) facet of graphite [40]. The size distribution in Fig. 1b ranges from 10.1 to 18.2nm, and the average diameter is ca. 13.6nm. The TEM images and size distribution of G-CDs, T-CDs and C-CDs show barely noticeable difference to A-CDs (Fig. S1). The UV–Vis spectra of A-CDs, G-CDs, T-CDs and C-CDs in Fig. 1c and Fig. S2 show typical optical absorption in the UV region. Since the precursor structure of CDs is similar to the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, the absorption peak of the nucleobase-based CDs at about 240nm represents the typical π–π* transition of C˭C bonds. As with the other CDs in literatures, the absorption spectrum of G-CDs shows another peak at 280nm, which is ascribed to the n-π* transition of C˭O bonds [41]. All the nucleobases did not show fluorescence emission, whereas their resultant CDs showed strong excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission (Fig. 1d, Fig. S3 and Fig. S4). Along with the increase of excitation wavelengths, the maximal PL emission of CDs gradually red-shifted. The maximal PL emission intensity increased at first and then reached an extremum before gradually decreasing until disappeared. Therefore, there exist an optimum excitation wavelength and a maximal emission peak intensity corresponding to each kind of nucleobase-based CDs. The PL emission spectra of A-CDs excited by various excitation wavelengths progressively from 300 to 370nm with 10nm increments are shown in Fig. 1d. A-CDs show a maximal PL emission located at ca. 420nm (λex = 320nm).

The excitation-dependent PL spectra reflected not only the size effects of A-CDs but also a considerable distribution of emissive trap sites on each CD [6,41]. The quantum yield of A-CDs, G-CDs, T-CDs and C-CDs was checked as 15.1%, 28.3%, 10.6% and 11.7%, respectively, which are significantly higher than that of previously reported DNA bio-dots (3.65%) [42]. Comparatively speaking, purine-based CDs possess a higher QY than pyrimidine-based CDs, which may be due to their carbon source with more N content. Considering the quantum confinement and edge effect, N-doping is an effective route to tune the electronic characteristics of CDs, which can improve the QY of CDs [36].

Based on the principle of complementary base pairing, the nucleobase groups on CD surface could selectively bind with corresponding complementary bases through hydrogen-bond interaction. The binding action could result in fluorescence quenching of CDs. In order to demonstrate the high selectivity of the CD-based chemosensor to nucleobases, we selected some analytes with similar chemical structure to nucleobases as contrast, including adenosine triphosphate, adenosine diphosphate, melamine, phthalic acid and ethylenediamine ( Fig. 2). We can see that A-CDs showed a selective response towards thymine and uracil compared with the other six analytes, which can reflect high selectivity of our sensor devices.

The as-prepared nucleobase-based CDs can realize quantitative determination of the corresponding nucleobases. As shown in Fig. 3, when quantitative thymine were added into A-CD solution, the fluorescence intensity of A-CDs decreased gradually. A good linear relationship existed between the fluorescence quenching rate of A-CDs and thymine concentrations (Fig. 3b). The calibration curve was (I0−I) / I = 0.01961 × CT(mM) + 0.01756 (R2 = 0.994). (I0−I) / I represent the fluorescence quenching rate of A-CDs in the presence of thymine, and CT is the thymine concentration. Given the fluorescence intensity variations, thymine concentration could be estimated at a range of 2–20mM according to the calibration curve. The detection limit could reach ca. 0.053mM based on a signal-to-noise ratio of three. Respectively, G-CDs, T-CDs and C-CDs can also realize the determination of their corresponding nucleobases (Fig. S5), and the experiments were conducted under the same conditions to A-CDs.

Compared with other fluorescence methods for nucleobase detection, the detection limit of the CD-based sensor is slightly higher and needs further improvement [32–35]. However, our method provides simple, convenient and satisfactory resolution to four kinds of nucleobases, which has not been reported in other fluorescence detecting systems.

To get insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was followed with or without their complementary bases to understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism. In Fig. 4, the significant decrease of the fluorescence lifetime of A-CDs from 5.58 to 3.34ns in the presence of thymine indicated that the linear fluorescence quenching of A-CDs with thymine from 0 to 20mM was a dynamic process and that energy transfer exists between A-CDs and thymine [43,44].

In this study, we evaluated the practical utility and stability of the CD sensor for nucleobase in real environments. Nucleobase assay in fetal calf serum was also conducted to evaluate the practical utility and stability of the nucleobase chemosensor on CDs ( Table 1). Results showed a relatively minor relative standard deviation (< 4.0%), indicating high accuracy and credibility of the sensing system.

To summarize, we reported a novel type of non-toxic CDs prepared from nucleobases using a facile hydrothermal strategy. The as-prepared nucleobase-based CDs showed selectively responsiveness to nucleobases. Compared with other fluorescence methods for nucleobase detection, the CD-based sensing platform can provide simple, convenient and satisfactory resolutions to four kinds of nucleobases, even though with a slightly higher detection limit. Giving the excellent sensitivity, preferable biocompatibility and nano-scale structure, the multifunctional sensing platform is adequate for intracellular imaging and sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51403093 and 51373073).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.05.071.
